stem: What is the sum of all [[n]] of the \( [[n]] \)th roots of unity (the [[n]] complex solutions of \( z^{[[n]]} = 1 \))?
solution: The roots are \( e^{2\pi i k/[[n]]} \) for \( k=0,\dots,[[n]]-1 \), a geometric series with ratio \( e^{2\pi i/[[n]]}\ne 1 \); by the geometric-series sum formula, \( \sum_{k=0}^{[[n]]-1} e^{2\pi i k/[[n]]} = \dfrac{e^{2\pi i}-1}{e^{2\pi i/[[n]]}-1} = [[answer]] \) (the roots are symmetric around the origin and cancel out).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of the trivial root \( z=1 \) alone, not the sum of all the roots.
This mistakes the number of roots for their sum.
This is the product of the roots when \( n \) is odd, not their sum.
This subtracts one from the root count for no valid reason.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: What is the product of all [[n]] of the \( [[n]] \)th roots of unity (the [[n]] complex solutions of \( z^{[[n]]} = 1 \))?
solution: By Vieta's formulas applied to \( x^{[[n]]}-1=0 \), the product of the [[n]] roots is \( (-1)^{[[n]]}\cdot\dfrac{-1}{1} = (-1)^{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is correct only when [[n]] is odd; it drops the sign that depends on the parity of [[n]].
This is correct only when [[n]] is even; it drops the sign that depends on the parity of [[n]].
This confuses the product of the roots with their sum, which is \( 0 \) for \( n \ge 2 \).
This mistakes the number of roots for their product.
This subtracts one from the root count for no valid reason.
The product of roots of unity always has modulus \( 1 \), so it can only be \( \pm 1 \), never \( 2 \).

stem: The \( [[n]] \)th roots of unity are \( e^{2\pi i k/[[n]]} \) for \( k=0,1,\dots,[[n]]-1 \). What is the root with \( k=[[k]] \), namely \( e^{2\pi i [[k]]/[[n]]} \), written in rectangular form \( x+yi \)?
solution: With angle \( \theta=\dfrac{2\pi[[k]]}{[[n]]} \), the root is \( \cos\theta + i\sin\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps sine and cosine; the real part of the root is \( \cos\theta \).
This is the conjugate root \( e^{-2\pi ik/n} \); the sign of the imaginary part was flipped.
This is the diametrically opposite point \( -e^{2\pi ik/n} \) on the unit circle.
This rotates the correct root by an extra quarter turn (multiplying by \( i \)).
This is off by one in the real part; every root of unity has modulus exactly 1.
This is off by one in the real part; every root of unity has modulus exactly 1.

stem: How many of the \( [[n]] \)th roots of unity (the [[n]] complex solutions of \( z^{[[n]]} = 1 \)) are real numbers?
solution: A root of unity \( e^{i\theta} \) is real exactly when \( \theta=0 \) or \( \theta=\pi \); the second occurs only when [[n]] is even (giving the root \( -1 \)). So there are 2 real roots when [[n]] is even and 1 (just \( z=1 \)) when [[n]] is odd: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is correct only when [[n]] is odd; it misses the extra real root \( -1 \) that appears when [[n]] is even.
This mistakes the total root count for the count of REAL roots.
This is not even always an integer, and does not correspond to any correct count of real roots.
This subtracts one from the total root count for no valid reason.
There are never more than 2 real roots of unity, since a real number on the unit circle must be \( \pm 1 \).
This misses the trivial real root \( z=1 \), which always exists.
